aggregating these model parameters. Moreover, practical analysis of losses
illustrates that the training loss for easily memorable images tends to be
obviously lower. Thus, we propose an anti-gradient control method to exclude
the sample with a lower loss value from the current mini-batch to avoid
memorizing. Extensive experiments and analysis on four datasets are conducted
to illustrate the effectiveness of our method, and results show that our method
successfully reduces memory capacity while even improving the performance
slightly. Moreover, to save the computing cost, we successfully apply our
method to fine-tune the well-trained diffusion models by limited epochs,
demonstrating the applicability of our method. Code is available in
https://github.com/liuxiao-guan/IET_AGC.
selected_base_paper_info.main_contributions: The paper addresses the memorization problem in diffusion models, presenting the IET-AGC framework that mitigates memorization while maintaining or slightly improving image generation quality. It demonstrates significant reductions in memorization metrics across several datasets.
selected_base_paper_info.methodology: The methodology involves an Iterative Ensemble Training (IET) framework that splits data into shards for training multiple models and periodically aggregates their parameters. Additionally, an Anti-Gradient Control (AGC) mechanism dynamically excludes samples with lower loss values from training to minimize memorization.
selected_base_paper_info.experimental_setup: The experiments utilized datasets such as CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, AFHQ-DOG for unconditional generation, and LAION-10k for text-conditioned generation. The effectiveness of the method was evaluated using metrics such as memorization quantity (MQ) and FID scores, with specific thresholds set for loss values to determine which samples to skip during training.
selected_base_paper_info.limitations: The choice of threshold λ, which balances memorization reduction and image quality, relies on experimental validation and lacks a systematic method for selection. The impact of varying parameters on model performance also remains a challenge.
selected_base_paper_info.future_research_directions: Future research could develop a method to quickly analyze datasets for optimal threshold selection and explore further improvements in the IET-AGC framework for diverse applications in data privacy protection.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Discffusion: Discriminative Diffusion Models as Few-shot Vision and
  Language Learners
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Diffusion models, such as Stable Diffusion, have shown incredible performance
on text-to-image generation. Since text-to-image generation often requires
models to generate visual concepts with fine-grained details and attributes
specified in text prompts, can we leverage the powerful representations learned
by pre-trained diffusion models for discriminative tasks such as image-text
matching? To answer this question, we propose a novel approach, Discriminative
Stable Diffusion (DSD), which turns pre-trained text-to-image diffusion models
into few-shot discriminative learners. Our approach mainly uses the
cross-attention score of a Stable Diffusion model to capture the mutual
influence between visual and textual information and fine-tune the model via
efficient attention-based prompt learning to perform image-text matching. By
comparing DSD with state-of-the-art methods on several benchmark datasets, we
demonstrate the potential of using pre-trained diffusion models for
discriminative tasks with superior results on few-shot image-text matching.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents a novel approach called Discriminative Stable Diffusion (Discffusion) that repurposes pre-trained diffusion models for discriminative tasks like image-text matching, showing superior performance over existing methods in few-shot conditions.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: Discffusion employs cross-attention scores from the Stable Diffusion model to gauge the alignment between image and text, along with attention-based prompt learning to fine-tune the model under few-shot learning scenarios.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments utilize datasets such as Compositional Visual Genome (ComVG) and RefCOCOg for image-text matching, and VQAv2 for visual question answering, evaluating performance through accuracy metrics and conducting comparisons against the CLIP model.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The approach relies heavily on pre-trained diffusion models, which might not be accessible for some applications and may have performance affected by the quality of the pre-training. Additionally, the model's capacity in understanding compositionality is not fully guaranteed.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could investigate the integration of multi-task learning techniques, explore better pre-training methods for diffusion models, and adapt this approach for other discriminative tasks beyond image-text matching.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Rethinking Video Deblurring with Wavelet-Aware Dynamic Transformer and
  Diffusion Model
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Current video deblurring methods have limitations in recovering
high-frequency information since the regression losses are conservative with
high-frequency details. Since Diffusion Models (DMs) have strong capabilities
in generating high-frequency details, we consider introducing DMs into the
video deblurring task. However, we found that directly applying DMs to the
video deblurring task has the following problems: (1) DMs require many
iteration steps to generate videos from Gaussian noise, which consumes many
computational resources. (2) DMs are easily misled by the blurry artifacts in
the video, resulting in irrational content and distortion of the deblurred
video. To address the above issues, we propose a novel video deblurring
framework VD-Diff that integrates the diffusion model into the Wavelet-Aware
Dynamic Transformer (WADT). Specifically, we perform the diffusion model in a
highly compact latent space to generate prior features containing
high-frequency information that conforms to the ground truth distribution. We
design the WADT to preserve and recover the low-frequency information in the
video while utilizing the high-frequency information generated by the diffusion
model. Extensive experiments show that our proposed VD-Diff outperforms SOTA
methods on GoPro, DVD, BSD, and Real-World Video datasets.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents VD-Diff, a novel framework for video deblurring that combines a Wavelet-Aware Dynamic Transformer (WADT) with a diffusion model (DM) to effectively recover high-frequency details that traditional methods struggle with, achieving state-of-the-art results on several datasets.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: VD-Diff employs a diffusion model in a compact latent space to generate high-frequency prior features and a WADT to capture and preserve low-frequency information from video frames, ultimately leading to clearer deblurred outputs.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments utilized datasets such as GoPro, DVD, BSD, and Real-World Video datasets, comparing the performance of VD-Diff against several state-of-the-art methods in terms of PSNR and SSIM metrics.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The paper notes that while diffusion models are effective, they are resource-intensive and can be misled by blurry artifacts, potentially resulting in distorted outputs if not integrated properly with the WADT.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could explore further optimization of the diffusion process, application of the framework to other video restoration tasks, and real-time implementation strategies to reduce computational load.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Noise Calibration: Plug-and-play Content-Preserving Video Enhancement
  using Pre-trained Video Diffusion Models
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
In order to improve the quality of synthesized videos, currently, one
predominant method involves retraining an expert diffusion model and then
implementing a noising-denoising process for refinement. Despite the
significant training costs, maintaining consistency of content between the
original and enhanced videos remains a major challenge. To tackle this
challenge, we propose a novel formulation that considers both visual quality
and consistency of content. Consistency of content is ensured by a proposed
loss function that maintains the structure of the input, while visual quality
is improved by utilizing the denoising process of pretrained diffusion models.
To address the formulated optimization problem, we have developed a
plug-and-play noise optimization strategy, referred to as Noise Calibration. By
refining the initial random noise through a few iterations, the content of
original video can be largely preserved, and the enhancement effect
demonstrates a notable improvement. Extensive experiments have demonstrated the
effectiveness of the proposed method.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The research introduces Noise Calibration, a novel formulation for video enhancement that addresses both visual quality and content consistency using pre-trained video diffusion models, thereby improving the enhancement of videos while preserving their original content.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The Noise Calibration method utilizes a proposed loss function to ensure content consistency during the enhancement process, leveraging pre-trained T2V diffusion models in a plug-and-play noise optimization strategy that involves refining initial random noise through a few iterations.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using the VideoCrafter model with a reference dataset of 700 videos at a resolution of 320x512. Various metrics were used for evaluation, including MSEl, MSE, SSIM, DOVER, and CLIP-IQA. The study compared NC-SDEdit against SDEdit to assess performance improvements.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The effectiveness of the Noise Calibration method is limited by the performance of the underlying base model, similar to SDEdit. Additionally, excessive iteration steps can lead to oversaturation of video colors.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could explore designing more effective consistency objectives to mitigate color oversaturation and extend the applicability of the Noise Calibration method to other generative tasks beyond video enhancement.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
GenSelfDiff-HIS: Generative Self-Supervision Using Diffusion for
  Histopathological Image Segmentation
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Histopathological image segmentation is a laborious and time-intensive task,
often requiring analysis from experienced pathologists for accurate
examinations. To reduce this burden, supervised machine-learning approaches
have been adopted using large-scale annotated datasets for histopathological
image analysis. However, in several scenarios, the availability of large-scale
annotated data is a bottleneck while training such models. Self-supervised
learning (SSL) is an alternative paradigm that provides some respite by
constructing models utilizing only the unannotated data which is often
abundant. The basic idea of SSL is to train a network to perform one or many
pseudo or pretext tasks on unannotated data and use it subsequently as the
basis for a variety of downstream tasks. It is seen that the success of SSL
depends critically on the considered pretext task. While there have been many
efforts in designing pretext tasks for classification problems, there haven't
been many attempts on SSL for histopathological segmentation. Motivated by
this, we propose an SSL approach for segmenting histopathological images via
generative diffusion models in this paper. Our method is based on the
observation that diffusion models effectively solve an image-to-image
translation task akin to a segmentation task. Hence, we propose generative
diffusion as the pretext task for histopathological image segmentation. We also
propose a multi-loss function-based fine-tuning for the downstream task. We
validate our method using several metrics on two publically available datasets
along with a newly proposed head and neck (HN) cancer dataset containing
hematoxylin and eosin (H\&E) stained images along with annotations. Codes will
be made public at https://github.com/suhas-srinath/GenSelfDiff-HIS.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper proposes a novel self-supervised learning (SSL) method for histopathological image segmentation using generative diffusion models, addressing the challenge of limited annotated datasets. The contributions include a new head and neck cancer dataset with annotated and unannotated images and demonstrating improved segmentation performance compared to existing methods through the use of denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) as a pretext task.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves utilizing a UNet-based diffusion model to perform self-supervised pre-training on unannotated histopathological images, followed by fine-tuning on labeled images using a multi-loss function that combines structural similarity and focal loss. The approach leverages DDPMs, which are effective for image-to-image tasks, making them suitable for SSL and subsequent segmentation.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments utilize three datasets: the newly proposed head and neck cancer dataset, Gland segmentation in colon histology images (GlaS), and multi-organ nucleus segmentation (MoNuSeg). The performance is evaluated using metrics such as Aggregated Jaccard Index (AJI), Intersection over Union (IoU), Hausdorff Distance (HD), and F1-score. The validation involves comparing against fully supervised models and various SSL pretext tasks on these datasets.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: Limitations include possible overfitting due to reliance on unannotated data and the assumption that the unlabeled images are of sufficient quality and diversity for effective learning. The paper does not address the potential variability in performance across different institutions or imaging conditions, nor does it explore the transferability of the learned models to entirely different datasets outside of the evaluated ones.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include enhancing SSL with segmentation-specific losses, exploring auxiliary tasks, and extending the method beyond segmentation tasks to include object detection or classification challenges in histopathological images.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
GLAD: Towards Better Reconstruction with Global and Local Adaptive
  Diffusion Models for Unsupervised Anomaly Detection
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Diffusion models have shown superior performance on unsupervised anomaly
detection tasks. Since trained with normal data only, diffusion models tend to
reconstruct normal counterparts of test images with certain noises added.
However, these methods treat all potential anomalies equally, which may cause
two main problems. From the global perspective, the difficulty of
reconstructing images with different anomalies is uneven. Therefore, instead of
utilizing the same setting for all samples, we propose to predict a particular
denoising step for each sample by evaluating the difference between image
contents and the priors extracted from diffusion models. From the local
perspective, reconstructing abnormal regions differs from normal areas even in
the same image. Theoretically, the diffusion model predicts a noise for each
step, typically following a standard Gaussian distribution. However, due to the
difference between the anomaly and its potential normal counterpart, the
predicted noise in abnormal regions will inevitably deviate from the standard
Gaussian distribution. To this end, we propose introducing synthetic abnormal
samples in training to encourage the diffusion models to break through the
limitation of standard Gaussian distribution, and a spatial-adaptive feature
fusion scheme is utilized during inference. With the above modifications, we
propose a global and local adaptive diffusion model (abbreviated to GLAD) for
unsupervised anomaly detection, which introduces appealing flexibility and
achieves anomaly-free reconstruction while retaining as much normal information
as possible. Extensive experiments are conducted on three commonly used anomaly
detection datasets (MVTec-AD, MPDD, and VisA) and a printed circuit board
dataset (PCB-Bank) we integrated, showing the effectiveness of the proposed
method.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents GLAD, a global and local adaptive diffusion model for unsupervised anomaly detection, addressing the problem of varying reconstruction difficulties for different types of anomalies. Key contributions include an Adaptive Denoising Step (ADP) for improved reconstruction flexibility based on the specific artifacts in images, an Anomaly-Oriented Training Paradigm (ATP) that fabricates synthetic abnormal samples to enhance training, and a Spatial-Adaptive Feature Fusion (SAFF) scheme to combine features for enhanced detail preservation during inference.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The GLAD method utilizes adaptive denoising steps adjusted dynamically for each sample based on their content. A noise weight is introduced to guide the reconstruction process, complemented by an ATP that synthesizes abnormal samples during training to help the model learn to predict non-Gaussian noise. Additionally, during inference, the SAFF selectively fuses features from reconstructed and original images to minimize residual artifacts and anomalies.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted on four datasets: MVTec-AD, MPDD, VisA, and an integrated PCB-Bank dataset. Various performance metrics were employed, including image-level and pixel-level Area Under the Receiver Operating Curve (AUROC) for anomaly detection and localization, respectively. Training involved fine-tuning a pre-trained diffusion model and applying augmentation strategies on normal samples while utilizing additional synthetic anomalies during the training process.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: Despite its advancements, GLAD's adaptive denoising may introduce added time complexity due to the iterative step comparison required to determine the optimal steps for each image. The method's reliance on synthesized abnormal samples during training implies potential deviations from real-world anomalies, potentially limiting the model's generalization. Moreover, the method may require extensive computational resources for adaptation and fine-tuning.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could focus on optimizing the evaluation procedures to reduce computational costs, developing techniques to predict denoising steps with fewer iterations, and exploring the integration of advanced machine learning methods to enhance the model's robustness against diverse real-world anomaly types.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
DPR: Diffusion Preference-based Reward for Offline Reinforcement
  Learning
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Offline preference-based reinforcement learning (PbRL) mitigates the need for
reward definition, aligning with human preferences via preference-driven reward
feedback without interacting with the environment. However, the effectiveness
of preference-driven reward functions depends on the modeling ability of the
learning model, which current MLP-based and Transformer-based methods may fail
to adequately provide. To alleviate the failure of the reward function caused
by insufficient modeling, we propose a novel preference-based reward
acquisition method: Diffusion Preference-based Reward (DPR). Unlike previous
methods using Bradley-Terry models for trajectory preferences, we use diffusion
models to directly model preference distributions for state-action pairs,
allowing rewards to be discriminatively obtained from these distributions. In
addition, considering the particularity of preference data that only know the
internal relationships of paired trajectories, we further propose Conditional
Diffusion Preference-based Reward (C-DPR), which leverages relative preference
information to enhance the construction of the diffusion model. We apply the
above methods to existing offline reinforcement learning algorithms and a
series of experiment results demonstrate that the diffusion-based reward
acquisition approach outperforms previous MLP-based and Transformer-based
methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The research introduces two novel methods for deriving preference-based rewards in offline reinforcement learning: Diffusion Preference-based Reward (DPR) and Conditional Diffusion Preference-based Reward (C-DPR). These methods leverage diffusion models to more effectively model preference distributions and obtain rewards for state-action pairs without the limitations of traditional MLP-based and Transformer-based approaches.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves using diffusion models to directly model preference distributions for state-action pairs, allowing for more robust reward acquisition. C-DPR additionally incorporates conditional preference information to enhance reward learning stability and accuracy.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using datasets from the Uni-RLHF platform, which includes preference data from Gym-MuJoCo and Adroit environments. The performance of the proposed methods was evaluated against existing offline reinforcement learning algorithms such as CQL, IQL, and TD3BC using crowdsourced and scripted teacher preference labels.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The study acknowledges that while diffusion models improve reward learning, simply categorizing rewards into two distributions may overlook nuanced preferences, leading to potential deviations from actual rewards. The effectiveness of C-DPR in various offline RL algorithms also requires careful evaluation based on algorithm choices.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could focus on refining the reward categorization process to capture a broader range of preferences, develop methods to explain why diffusion models outperform traditional approaches, and explore further applications of the proposed reward mechanisms in different reinforcement learning settings.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
EMDM: Efficient Motion Diffusion Model for Fast and High-Quality Motion
  Generation
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
We introduce Efficient Motion Diffusion Model (EMDM) for fast and
high-quality human motion generation. Current state-of-the-art generative
diffusion models have produced impressive results but struggle to achieve fast
generation without sacrificing quality. On the one hand, previous works, like
motion latent diffusion, conduct diffusion within a latent space for
efficiency, but learning such a latent space can be a non-trivial effort. On
the other hand, accelerating generation by naively increasing the sampling step
size, e.g., DDIM, often leads to quality degradation as it fails to approximate
the complex denoising distribution. To address these issues, we propose EMDM,
which captures the complex distribution during multiple sampling steps in the
diffusion model, allowing for much fewer sampling steps and significant
acceleration in generation. This is achieved by a conditional denoising
diffusion GAN to capture multimodal data distributions among arbitrary (and
potentially larger) step sizes conditioned on control signals, enabling
fewer-step motion sampling with high fidelity and diversity. To minimize
undesired motion artifacts, geometric losses are imposed during network
learning. As a result, EMDM achieves real-time motion generation and
significantly improves the efficiency of motion diffusion models compared to
existing methods while achieving high-quality motion generation. Our code will
be publicly available upon publication.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The Efficient Motion Diffusion Model (EMDM) addresses the challenge of achieving fast motion generation without sacrificing quality in human motion synthesis. It captures complex motion distributions allowing for fewer sampling steps, significantly accelerating the generation process and enhancing fidelity and diversity compared to existing methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: EMDM utilizes a conditional denoising diffusion GAN architecture that integrates a conditional generator and discriminator. It captures complex multimodal distributions based on input control signals, facilitating fewer sampling steps during denoising. The model is trained end-to-end and incorporates geometric loss functions to minimize motion artifacts.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using datasets including HumanML3D, KIT, and HumanAct12 for text-to-motion and action-to-motion tasks. Various metrics such as Frechet Inception Distance (FID) and motion diversity were employed for evaluation, focusing on motion quality, diversity, and runtime performance. The model's inference efficiency was compared against state-of-the-art methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: EMDM lacks physical considerations, potentially leading to issues like floating and ground penetration in generated motions. Additionally, it primarily accepts textual inputs, limiting its applicability to visual inputs or other modalities.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could explore integrating physics-based constraints to improve motion realism, and extending the model to accept visual or audio inputs for more versatile applications in motion synthesis.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper proposes a novel self-supervised learning (SSL) method for histopathological image segmentation using generative diffusion models, addressing the challenge of limited annotated datasets. The contributions include a new head and neck cancer dataset with annotated and unannotated images and demonstrating improved segmentation performance compared to existing methods through the use of denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) as a pretext task.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves utilizing a UNet-based diffusion model to perform self-supervised pre-training on unannotated histopathological images, followed by fine-tuning on labeled images using a multi-loss function that combines structural similarity and focal loss. The approach leverages DDPMs, which are effective for image-to-image tasks, making them suitable for SSL and subsequent segmentation.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments utilize three datasets: the newly proposed head and neck cancer dataset, Gland segmentation in colon histology images (GlaS), and multi-organ nucleus segmentation (MoNuSeg). The performance is evaluated using metrics such as Aggregated Jaccard Index (AJI), Intersection over Union (IoU), Hausdorff Distance (HD), and F1-score. The validation involves comparing against fully supervised models and various SSL pretext tasks on these datasets.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: Limitations include possible overfitting due to reliance on unannotated data and the assumption that the unlabeled images are of sufficient quality and diversity for effective learning. The paper does not address the potential variability in performance across different institutions or imaging conditions, nor does it explore the transferability of the learned models to entirely different datasets outside of the evaluated ones.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include enhancing SSL with segmentation-specific losses, exploring auxiliary tasks, and extending the method beyond segmentation tasks to include object detection or classification challenges in histopathological images.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
GLAD: Towards Better Reconstruction with Global and Local Adaptive
  Diffusion Models for Unsupervised Anomaly Detection
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Diffusion models have shown superior performance on unsupervised anomaly
detection tasks. Since trained with normal data only, diffusion models tend to
reconstruct normal counterparts of test images with certain noises added.
However, these methods treat all potential anomalies equally, which may cause
two main problems. From the global perspective, the difficulty of
reconstructing images with different anomalies is uneven. Therefore, instead of
utilizing the same setting for all samples, we propose to predict a particular
denoising step for each sample by evaluating the difference between image
contents and the priors extracted from diffusion models. From the local
perspective, reconstructing abnormal regions differs from normal areas even in
the same image. Theoretically, the diffusion model predicts a noise for each
step, typically following a standard Gaussian distribution. However, due to the
difference between the anomaly and its potential normal counterpart, the
predicted noise in abnormal regions will inevitably deviate from the standard
Gaussian distribution. To this end, we propose introducing synthetic abnormal
samples in training to encourage the diffusion models to break through the
limitation of standard Gaussian distribution, and a spatial-adaptive feature
fusion scheme is utilized during inference. With the above modifications, we
propose a global and local adaptive diffusion model (abbreviated to GLAD) for
unsupervised anomaly detection, which introduces appealing flexibility and
achieves anomaly-free reconstruction while retaining as much normal information
as possible. Extensive experiments are conducted on three commonly used anomaly
detection datasets (MVTec-AD, MPDD, and VisA) and a printed circuit board
dataset (PCB-Bank) we integrated, showing the effectiveness of the proposed
method.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents GLAD, a global and local adaptive diffusion model for unsupervised anomaly detection, addressing the problem of varying reconstruction difficulties for different types of anomalies. Key contributions include an Adaptive Denoising Step (ADP) for improved reconstruction flexibility based on the specific artifacts in images, an Anomaly-Oriented Training Paradigm (ATP) that fabricates synthetic abnormal samples to enhance training, and a Spatial-Adaptive Feature Fusion (SAFF) scheme to combine features for enhanced detail preservation during inference.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The GLAD method utilizes adaptive denoising steps adjusted dynamically for each sample based on their content. A noise weight is introduced to guide the reconstruction process, complemented by an ATP that synthesizes abnormal samples during training to help the model learn to predict non-Gaussian noise. Additionally, during inference, the SAFF selectively fuses features from reconstructed and original images to minimize residual artifacts and anomalies.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted on four datasets: MVTec-AD, MPDD, VisA, and an integrated PCB-Bank dataset. Various performance metrics were employed, including image-level and pixel-level Area Under the Receiver Operating Curve (AUROC) for anomaly detection and localization, respectively. Training involved fine-tuning a pre-trained diffusion model and applying augmentation strategies on normal samples while utilizing additional synthetic anomalies during the training process.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: Despite its advancements, GLAD's adaptive denoising may introduce added time complexity due to the iterative step comparison required to determine the optimal steps for each image. The method's reliance on synthesized abnormal samples during training implies potential deviations from real-world anomalies, potentially limiting the model's generalization. Moreover, the method may require extensive computational resources for adaptation and fine-tuning.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could focus on optimizing the evaluation procedures to reduce computational costs, developing techniques to predict denoising steps with fewer iterations, and exploring the integration of advanced machine learning methods to enhance the model's robustness against diverse real-world anomaly types.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
DPR: Diffusion Preference-based Reward for Offline Reinforcement
  Learning
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Offline preference-based reinforcement learning (PbRL) mitigates the need for
reward definition, aligning with human preferences via preference-driven reward
feedback without interacting with the environment. However, the effectiveness
of preference-driven reward functions depends on the modeling ability of the
learning model, which current MLP-based and Transformer-based methods may fail
to adequately provide. To alleviate the failure of the reward function caused
by insufficient modeling, we propose a novel preference-based reward
acquisition method: Diffusion Preference-based Reward (DPR). Unlike previous
methods using Bradley-Terry models for trajectory preferences, we use diffusion
models to directly model preference distributions for state-action pairs,
allowing rewards to be discriminatively obtained from these distributions. In
addition, considering the particularity of preference data that only know the
internal relationships of paired trajectories, we further propose Conditional
Diffusion Preference-based Reward (C-DPR), which leverages relative preference
information to enhance the construction of the diffusion model. We apply the
above methods to existing offline reinforcement learning algorithms and a
series of experiment results demonstrate that the diffusion-based reward
acquisition approach outperforms previous MLP-based and Transformer-based
methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The research introduces two novel methods for deriving preference-based rewards in offline reinforcement learning: Diffusion Preference-based Reward (DPR) and Conditional Diffusion Preference-based Reward (C-DPR). These methods leverage diffusion models to more effectively model preference distributions and obtain rewards for state-action pairs without the limitations of traditional MLP-based and Transformer-based approaches.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves using diffusion models to directly model preference distributions for state-action pairs, allowing for more robust reward acquisition. C-DPR additionally incorporates conditional preference information to enhance reward learning stability and accuracy.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using datasets from the Uni-RLHF platform, which includes preference data from Gym-MuJoCo and Adroit environments. The performance of the proposed methods was evaluated against existing offline reinforcement learning algorithms such as CQL, IQL, and TD3BC using crowdsourced and scripted teacher preference labels.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The study acknowledges that while diffusion models improve reward learning, simply categorizing rewards into two distributions may overlook nuanced preferences, leading to potential deviations from actual rewards. The effectiveness of C-DPR in various offline RL algorithms also requires careful evaluation based on algorithm choices.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could focus on refining the reward categorization process to capture a broader range of preferences, develop methods to explain why diffusion models outperform traditional approaches, and explore further applications of the proposed reward mechanisms in different reinforcement learning settings.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
EMDM: Efficient Motion Diffusion Model for Fast and High-Quality Motion
  Generation
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
We introduce Efficient Motion Diffusion Model (EMDM) for fast and
high-quality human motion generation. Current state-of-the-art generative
diffusion models have produced impressive results but struggle to achieve fast
generation without sacrificing quality. On the one hand, previous works, like
motion latent diffusion, conduct diffusion within a latent space for
efficiency, but learning such a latent space can be a non-trivial effort. On
the other hand, accelerating generation by naively increasing the sampling step
size, e.g., DDIM, often leads to quality degradation as it fails to approximate
the complex denoising distribution. To address these issues, we propose EMDM,
which captures the complex distribution during multiple sampling steps in the
diffusion model, allowing for much fewer sampling steps and significant
acceleration in generation. This is achieved by a conditional denoising
diffusion GAN to capture multimodal data distributions among arbitrary (and
potentially larger) step sizes conditioned on control signals, enabling
fewer-step motion sampling with high fidelity and diversity. To minimize
undesired motion artifacts, geometric losses are imposed during network
learning. As a result, EMDM achieves real-time motion generation and
significantly improves the efficiency of motion diffusion models compared to
existing methods while achieving high-quality motion generation. Our code will
be publicly available upon publication.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The Efficient Motion Diffusion Model (EMDM) addresses the challenge of achieving fast motion generation without sacrificing quality in human motion synthesis. It captures complex motion distributions allowing for fewer sampling steps, significantly accelerating the generation process and enhancing fidelity and diversity compared to existing methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: EMDM utilizes a conditional denoising diffusion GAN architecture that integrates a conditional generator and discriminator. It captures complex multimodal distributions based on input control signals, facilitating fewer sampling steps during denoising. The model is trained end-to-end and incorporates geometric loss functions to minimize motion artifacts.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using datasets including HumanML3D, KIT, and HumanAct12 for text-to-motion and action-to-motion tasks. Various metrics such as Frechet Inception Distance (FID) and motion diversity were employed for evaluation, focusing on motion quality, diversity, and runtime performance. The model's inference efficiency was compared against state-of-the-art methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: EMDM lacks physical considerations, potentially leading to issues like floating and ground penetration in generated motions. Additionally, it primarily accepts textual inputs, limiting its applicability to visual inputs or other modalities.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could explore integrating physics-based constraints to improve motion realism, and extending the model to accept visual or audio inputs for more versatile applications in motion synthesis.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title:
Discffusion: Discriminative Diffusion Models as Few-shot Vision and
  Language Learners
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
Diffusion models, such as Stable Diffusion, have shown incredible performance
on text-to-image generation. Since text-to-image generation often requires
models to generate visual concepts with fine-grained details and attributes
specified in text prompts, can we leverage the powerful representations learned
by pre-trained diffusion models for discriminative tasks such as image-text
matching? To answer this question, we propose a novel approach, Discriminative
Stable Diffusion (DSD), which turns pre-trained text-to-image diffusion models
into few-shot discriminative learners. Our approach mainly uses the
cross-attention score of a Stable Diffusion model to capture the mutual
influence between visual and textual information and fine-tune the model via
efficient attention-based prompt learning to perform image-text matching. By
comparing DSD with state-of-the-art methods on several benchmark datasets, we
demonstrate the potential of using pre-trained diffusion models for
discriminative tasks with superior results on few-shot image-text matching.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents a novel approach called Discriminative Stable Diffusion (Discffusion) that repurposes pre-trained diffusion models for discriminative tasks like image-text matching, showing superior performance over existing methods in few-shot conditions.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: Discffusion employs cross-attention scores from the Stable Diffusion model to gauge the alignment between image and text, along with attention-based prompt learning to fine-tune the model under few-shot learning scenarios.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments utilize datasets such as Compositional Visual Genome (ComVG) and RefCOCOg for image-text matching, and VQAv2 for visual question answering, evaluating performance through accuracy metrics and conducting comparisons against the CLIP model.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The approach relies heavily on pre-trained diffusion models, which might not be accessible for some applications and may have performance affected by the quality of the pre-training. Additionally, the model's capacity in understanding compositionality is not fully guaranteed.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could investigate the integration of multi-task learning techniques, explore better pre-training methods for diffusion models, and adapt this approach for other discriminative tasks beyond image-text matching.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title:
Rethinking Video Deblurring with Wavelet-Aware Dynamic Transformer and
  Diffusion Model
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
Current video deblurring methods have limitations in recovering
high-frequency information since the regression losses are conservative with
high-frequency details. Since Diffusion Models (DMs) have strong capabilities
in generating high-frequency details, we consider introducing DMs into the
video deblurring task. However, we found that directly applying DMs to the
video deblurring task has the following problems: (1) DMs require many
iteration steps to generate videos from Gaussian noise, which consumes many
computational resources. (2) DMs are easily misled by the blurry artifacts in
the video, resulting in irrational content and distortion of the deblurred
video. To address the above issues, we propose a novel video deblurring
framework VD-Diff that integrates the diffusion model into the Wavelet-Aware
Dynamic Transformer (WADT). Specifically, we perform the diffusion model in a
highly compact latent space to generate prior features containing
high-frequency information that conforms to the ground truth distribution. We
design the WADT to preserve and recover the low-frequency information in the
video while utilizing the high-frequency information generated by the diffusion
model. Extensive experiments show that our proposed VD-Diff outperforms SOTA
methods on GoPro, DVD, BSD, and Real-World Video datasets.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents VD-Diff, a novel framework for video deblurring that combines a Wavelet-Aware Dynamic Transformer (WADT) with a diffusion model (DM) to effectively recover high-frequency details that traditional methods struggle with, achieving state-of-the-art results on several datasets.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: VD-Diff employs a diffusion model in a compact latent space to generate high-frequency prior features and a WADT to capture and preserve low-frequency information from video frames, ultimately leading to clearer deblurred outputs.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments utilized datasets such as GoPro, DVD, BSD, and Real-World Video datasets, comparing the performance of VD-Diff against several state-of-the-art methods in terms of PSNR and SSIM metrics.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The paper notes that while diffusion models are effective, they are resource-intensive and can be misled by blurry artifacts, potentially resulting in distorted outputs if not integrated properly with the WADT.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could explore further optimization of the diffusion process, application of the framework to other video restoration tasks, and real-time implementation strategies to reduce computational load.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title:
Noise Calibration: Plug-and-play Content-Preserving Video Enhancement
  using Pre-trained Video Diffusion Models
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
In order to improve the quality of synthesized videos, currently, one
predominant method involves retraining an expert diffusion model and then
implementing a noising-denoising process for refinement. Despite the
significant training costs, maintaining consistency of content between the
original and enhanced videos remains a major challenge. To tackle this
challenge, we propose a novel formulation that considers both visual quality
and consistency of content. Consistency of content is ensured by a proposed
loss function that maintains the structure of the input, while visual quality
is improved by utilizing the denoising process of pretrained diffusion models.
To address the formulated optimization problem, we have developed a
plug-and-play noise optimization strategy, referred to as Noise Calibration. By
refining the initial random noise through a few iterations, the content of
original video can be largely preserved, and the enhancement effect
demonstrates a notable improvement. Extensive experiments have demonstrated the
effectiveness of the proposed method.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: The research introduces Noise Calibration, a novel formulation for video enhancement that addresses both visual quality and content consistency using pre-trained video diffusion models, thereby improving the enhancement of videos while preserving their original content.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: The Noise Calibration method utilizes a proposed loss function to ensure content consistency during the enhancement process, leveraging pre-trained T2V diffusion models in a plug-and-play noise optimization strategy that involves refining initial random noise through a few iterations.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using the VideoCrafter model with a reference dataset of 700 videos at a resolution of 320x512. Various metrics were used for evaluation, including MSEl, MSE, SSIM, DOVER, and CLIP-IQA. The study compared NC-SDEdit against SDEdit to assess performance improvements.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The effectiveness of the Noise Calibration method is limited by the performance of the underlying base model, similar to SDEdit. Additionally, excessive iteration steps can lead to oversaturation of video colors.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could explore designing more effective consistency objectives to mitigate color oversaturation and extend the applicability of the Noise Calibration method to other generative tasks beyond video enhancement.

{"arxiv_id":"2407.15328v3","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2407.15328v3","title":"Iterative Ensemble Training with Anti-Gradient Control for Mitigating
  Memorization in Diffusion Models","authors":["Xiao Liu","Xiaoliu Guan","Yu Wu","Jiaxu Miao"],"published_date":"2024-07-22T02:19:30Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Diffusion models, known for their tremendous ability to generate novel and
high-quality samples, have recently raised concerns due to their data
memorization behavior, which poses privacy risks. Recent approaches for memory
mitigation either only focused on the text modality problem in cross-modal
generation tasks or utilized data augmentation strategies. In this paper, we
propose a novel training framework for diffusion models from the perspective of
visual modality, which is more generic and fundamental for mitigating
memorization. To facilitate forgetting of stored information in diffusion model
parameters, we propose an iterative ensemble training strategy by splitting the
data into multiple shards for training multiple models and intermittently
aggregating these model parameters. Moreover, practical analysis of losses
illustrates that the training loss for easily memorable images tends to be
obviously lower. Thus, we propose an anti-gradient control method to exclude
the sample with a lower loss value from the current mini-batch to avoid
memorizing. Extensive experiments and analysis on four datasets are conducted
to illustrate the effectiveness of our method, and results show that our method
successfully reduces memory capacity while even improving the performance
slightly. Moreover, to save the computing cost, we successfully apply our
method to fine-tune the well-trained diffusion models by limited epochs,
demonstrating the applicability of our method. Code is available in
https://github.com/liuxiao-guan/IET_AGC.","github_url":"https://github.com/liuxiao-guan/IET_AGC","main_contributions":"The paper addresses the memorization problem in diffusion models, presenting the IET-AGC framework that mitigates memorization while maintaining or slightly improving image generation quality. It demonstrates significant reductions in memorization metrics across several datasets.","methodology":"The methodology involves an Iterative Ensemble Training (IET) framework that splits data into shards for training multiple models and periodically aggregates their parameters. Additionally, an Anti-Gradient Control (AGC) mechanism dynamically excludes samples with lower loss values from training to minimize memorization.","experimental_setup":"The experiments utilized datasets such as CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, AFHQ-DOG for unconditional generation, and LAION-10k for text-conditioned generation. The effectiveness of the method was evaluated using metrics such as memorization quantity (MQ) and FID scores, with specific thresholds set for loss values to determine which samples to skip during training.","limitations":"The choice of threshold λ, which balances memorization reduction and image quality, relies on experimental validation and lacks a systematic method for selection. The impact of varying parameters on model performance also remains a challenge.","future_research_directions":"Future research could develop a method to quickly analyze datasets for optimal threshold selection and explore further improvements in the IET-AGC framework for diverse applications in data privacy protection."}

model. Extensive experiments show that our proposed VD-Diff outperforms SOTA
methods on GoPro, DVD, BSD, and Real-World Video datasets.","github_url":"https://github.com/Chen-Rao/VD-Diff","main_contributions":"The paper presents VD-Diff, a novel framework for video deblurring that combines a Wavelet-Aware Dynamic Transformer (WADT) with a diffusion model (DM) to effectively recover high-frequency details that traditional methods struggle with, achieving state-of-the-art results on several datasets.","methodology":"VD-Diff employs a diffusion model in a compact latent space to generate high-frequency prior features and a WADT to capture and preserve low-frequency information from video frames, ultimately leading to clearer deblurred outputs.","experimental_setup":"The experiments utilized datasets such as GoPro, DVD, BSD, and Real-World Video datasets, comparing the performance of VD-Diff against several state-of-the-art methods in terms of PSNR and SSIM metrics.","limitations":"The paper notes that while diffusion models are effective, they are resource-intensive and can be misled by blurry artifacts, potentially resulting in distorted outputs if not integrated properly with the WADT.","future_research_directions":"Future work could explore further optimization of the diffusion process, application of the framework to other video restoration tasks, and real-time implementation strategies to reduce computational load."}

{"arxiv_id":"2407.10285v1","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2407.10285v1","title":"Noise Calibration: Plug-and-play Content-Preserving Video Enhancement
  using Pre-trained Video Diffusion Models","authors":["Qinyu Yang","Haoxin Chen","Yong Zhang","Menghan Xia","Xiaodong Cun","Zhixun Su","Ying Shan"],"published_date":"2024-07-14T17:59:56Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"In order to improve the quality of synthesized videos, currently, one
predominant method involves retraining an expert diffusion model and then
implementing a noising-denoising process for refinement. Despite the
significant training costs, maintaining consistency of content between the
original and enhanced videos remains a major challenge. To tackle this
challenge, we propose a novel formulation that considers both visual quality
and consistency of content. Consistency of content is ensured by a proposed
loss function that maintains the structure of the input, while visual quality
is improved by utilizing the denoising process of pretrained diffusion models.
To address the formulated optimization problem, we have developed a
plug-and-play noise optimization strategy, referred to as Noise Calibration. By
refining the initial random noise through a few iterations, the content of
original video can be largely preserved, and the enhancement effect
demonstrates a notable improvement. Extensive experiments have demonstrated the
effectiveness of the proposed method.","github_url":"https://github.com/yangqy1110/NC-SDEdit","main_contributions":"The research introduces Noise Calibration, a novel formulation for video enhancement that addresses both visual quality and content consistency using pre-trained video diffusion models, thereby improving the enhancement of videos while preserving their original content.","methodology":"The Noise Calibration method utilizes a proposed loss function to ensure content consistency during the enhancement process, leveraging pre-trained T2V diffusion models in a plug-and-play noise optimization strategy that involves refining initial random noise through a few iterations.","experimental_setup":"Experiments were conducted using the VideoCrafter model with a reference dataset of 700 videos at a resolution of 320x512. Various metrics were used for evaluation, including MSEl, MSE, SSIM, DOVER, and CLIP-IQA. The study compared NC-SDEdit against SDEdit to assess performance improvements.","limitations":"The effectiveness of the Noise Calibration method is limited by the performance of the underlying base model, similar to SDEdit. Additionally, excessive iteration steps can lead to oversaturation of video colors.","future_research_directions":"Future research could explore designing more effective consistency objectives to mitigate color oversaturation and extend the applicability of the Noise Calibration method to other generative tasks beyond video enhancement."}

{"arxiv_id":"2406.07487v3","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2406.07487v3","title":"GLAD: Towards Better Reconstruction with Global and Local Adaptive
  Diffusion Models for Unsupervised Anomaly Detection","authors":["Hang Yao","Ming Liu","Haolin Wang","Zhicun Yin","Zifei Yan","Xiaopeng Hong","Wangmeng Zuo"],"published_date":"2024-06-11T17:27:23Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Diffusion models have shown superior performance on unsupervised anomaly
detection tasks. Since trained with normal data only, diffusion models tend to
reconstruct normal counterparts of test images with certain noises added.
However, these methods treat all potential anomalies equally, which may cause
two main problems. From the global perspective, the difficulty of
reconstructing images with different anomalies is uneven. Therefore, instead of
utilizing the same setting for all samples, we propose to predict a particular
denoising step for each sample by evaluating the difference between image
contents and the priors extracted from diffusion models. From the local
perspective, reconstructing abnormal regions differs from normal areas even in
the same image. Theoretically, the diffusion model predicts a noise for each
step, typically following a standard Gaussian distribution. However, due to the
difference between the anomaly and its potential normal counterpart, the
predicted noise in abnormal regions will inevitably deviate from the standard
Gaussian distribution. To this end, we propose introducing synthetic abnormal
samples in training to encourage the diffusion models to break through the
limitation of standard Gaussian distribution, and a spatial-adaptive feature
fusion scheme is utilized during inference. With the above modifications, we
propose a global and local adaptive diffusion model (abbreviated to GLAD) for
unsupervised anomaly detection, which introduces appealing flexibility and
achieves anomaly-free reconstruction while retaining as much normal information
as possible. Extensive experiments are conducted on three commonly used anomaly
detection datasets (MVTec-AD, MPDD, and VisA) and a printed circuit board
dataset (PCB-Bank) we integrated, showing the effectiveness of the proposed
method.","github_url":"https://github.com/hyao1/GLAD","main_contributions":"The paper presents GLAD, a global and local adaptive diffusion model for unsupervised anomaly detection, addressing the problem of varying reconstruction difficulties for different types of anomalies. Key contributions include an Adaptive Denoising Step (ADP) for improved reconstruction flexibility based on the specific artifacts in images, an Anomaly-Oriented Training Paradigm (ATP) that fabricates synthetic abnormal samples to enhance training, and a Spatial-Adaptive Feature Fusion (SAFF) scheme to combine features for enhanced detail preservation during inference.","methodology":"The GLAD method utilizes adaptive denoising steps adjusted dynamically for each sample based on their content. A noise weight is introduced to guide the reconstruction process, complemented by an ATP that synthesizes abnormal samples during training to help the model learn to predict non-Gaussian noise. Additionally, during inference, the SAFF selectively fuses features from reconstructed and original images to minimize residual artifacts and anomalies.","experimental_setup":"Experiments were conducted on four datasets: MVTec-AD, MPDD, VisA, and an integrated PCB-Bank dataset. Various performance metrics were employed, including image-level and pixel-level Area Under the Receiver Operating Curve (AUROC) for anomaly detection and localization, respectively. Training involved fine-tuning a pre-trained diffusion model and applying augmentation strategies on normal samples while utilizing additional synthetic anomalies during the training process.","limitations":"Despite its advancements, GLAD's adaptive denoising may introduce added time complexity due to the iterative step comparison required to determine the optimal steps for each image. The method's reliance on synthesized abnormal samples during training implies potential deviations from real-world anomalies, potentially limiting the model's generalization. Moreover, the method may require extensive computational resources for adaptation and fine-tuning.","future_research_directions":"Future research could focus on optimizing the evaluation procedures to reduce computational costs, developing techniques to predict denoising steps with fewer iterations, and exploring the integration of advanced machine learning methods to enhance the model's robustness against diverse real-world anomaly types."}

{"arxiv_id":"2503.01143v1","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2503.01143v1","title":"DPR: Diffusion Preference-based Reward for Offline Reinforcement
  Learning","authors":["Teng Pang","Bingzheng Wang","Guoqiang Wu","Yilong Yin"],"published_date":"2025-03-03T03:49:38Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Offline preference-based reinforcement learning (PbRL) mitigates the need for
reward definition, aligning with human preferences via preference-driven reward
feedback without interacting with the environment. However, the effectiveness
of preference-driven reward functions depends on the modeling ability of the
learning model, which current MLP-based and Transformer-based methods may fail
to adequately provide. To alleviate the failure of the reward function caused
by insufficient modeling, we propose a novel preference-based reward
acquisition method: Diffusion Preference-based Reward (DPR). Unlike previous
methods using Bradley-Terry models for trajectory preferences, we use diffusion
models to directly model preference distributions for state-action pairs,
allowing rewards to be discriminatively obtained from these distributions. In
addition, considering the particularity of preference data that only know the
internal relationships of paired trajectories, we further propose Conditional
Diffusion Preference-based Reward (C-DPR), which leverages relative preference
information to enhance the construction of the diffusion model. We apply the
above methods to existing offline reinforcement learning algorithms and a
series of experiment results demonstrate that the diffusion-based reward
acquisition approach outperforms previous MLP-based and Transformer-based
methods.","github_url":"https://github.com/TU2021/DTR","main_contributions":"The research introduces two novel methods for deriving preference-based rewards in offline reinforcement learning: Diffusion Preference-based Reward (DPR) and Conditional Diffusion Preference-based Reward (C-DPR). These methods leverage diffusion models to more effectively model preference distributions and obtain rewards for state-action pairs without the limitations of traditional MLP-based and Transformer-based approaches.","methodology":"The methodology involves using diffusion models to directly model preference distributions for state-action pairs, allowing for more robust reward acquisition. C-DPR additionally incorporates conditional preference information to enhance reward learning stability and accuracy.","experimental_setup":"Experiments were conducted using datasets from the Uni-RLHF platform, which includes preference data from Gym-MuJoCo and Adroit environments. The performance of the proposed methods was evaluated against existing offline reinforcement learning algorithms such as CQL, IQL, and TD3BC using crowdsourced and scripted teacher preference labels.","limitations":"The study acknowledges that while diffusion models improve reward learning, simply categorizing rewards into two distributions may overlook nuanced preferences, leading to potential deviations from actual rewards. The effectiveness of C-DPR in various offline RL algorithms also requires careful evaluation based on algorithm choices.","future_research_directions":"Future research could focus on refining the reward categorization process to capture a broader range of preferences, develop methods to explain why diffusion models outperform traditional approaches, and explore further applications of the proposed reward mechanisms in different reinforcement learning settings."}
new_method:
Below is the outcome of step 3—a new method that marries the ensemble–anti‐gradient idea of the Base Method with a discriminative feedback mechanism inspired by one of the Add Methods.

──────────────────────────────
Dynamic Discriminative Anti‐Memorization Ensemble (D-DAME)

Overview:
D-DAME rethinks diffusion model training by merging iterative ensemble training and anti‐gradient control (from the Base Method) with a dynamic, discriminative feedback module inspired by approaches such as Discriminative Stable Diffusion. Instead of using a fixed, manually tuned loss threshold (λ) to decide which samples to “skip” during gradient updates, D-DAME deploys a learned threshold predictor that leverages cross-attention signals and few-shot discriminative cues. This module dynamically evaluates, for each training sample, its risk of being memorized. In doing so, it modulates gradient contributions (a “soft” anti‐memorization control) to protect image quality while strongly discouraging memorization.

Key Components:

1. Iterative Ensemble Training:
 • As in the Base Method, the training data are split into shards and multiple diffusion models are trained in parallel.
 • Periodically, model parameters are aggregated to encourage forgetting of overly memorized details and to prevent any single model from “overfitting” specific examples.

2. Discriminative Memory Risk Estimator (DMRE):
 • Inspired by discriminative approaches that leverage cross-attention (as seen in Discriminative Stable Diffusion), DMRE is a lightweight, auxiliary network that processes intermediate representations (including cross-attention maps) of the diffusion model.
 • Its goal is to predict, in a few-shot manner, a per-sample “memory risk score” that estimates how likely the current sample is to be memorized.
 • This score replaces the fixed threshold λ by defining a dynamic, sample-specific cutoff. For instance, the DMRE may label a sample as “high risk” if its aggregated attention or reconstruction metrics indicate unusually strong memorization tendencies (i.e. abnormally low loss relative to its content).

3. Adaptive Anti-Gradient Control:
 • Rather than outright excluding a sample (as in the Base Method’s anti-gradient control), D-DAME modulates its gradient contribution.
 • If DMRE flags a sample as high risk, its corresponding gradient is down-weighted (or “damped”) during backpropagation. For lower-risk samples, gradients remain largely intact.
 • The damping factor is learned and refined throughout training so that it automatically adapts to different data characteristics and training stages.

4. End-to-End Joint Optimization:
 • Both the ensemble diffusion models and the DMRE are optimized jointly. A small validation set with known memorization artifacts (or augmented “memorization-prone” synthetic examples) is used periodically to fine-tune the DMRE.
 • This joint optimization encourages the DMRE to become both sensitive and robust in detecting memorization “signals” from ongoing training.

Benefits:
 • Automated, dynamic thresholding removes the need for ad hoc manual selection of a fixed λ.
 • The discriminative signal (inspired by cross-attention based few-shot learning) provides a richer understanding of what constitutes memorization versus desired learning.
 • By “dampening” rather than excluding updates, the method offers a continuous trade-off between preserving image quality and reducing memorization.
 • The iterative ensemble strategy further reinforces forgetting of unwanted information since repeated parameter aggregations prevent any one model from “remembering” too strongly.

──────────────────────────────
In summary, D-DAME offers a truly novel training paradigm for diffusion models. It addresses one of the key challenges of the Base Method—the dependence on a hand-tuned, static loss threshold—by incorporating a discriminative module to dynamically assess memorization risk and modulate gradients accordingly, all while maintaining the strengths of the original ensemble training strategy.
